title: Cherry strudel
subtitle: Austrian filo-thin stretched pastry with a tart cherry filling
excerpt: An Austrian strudel made by stretching a simple flour-and-oil dough paper-thin over a cloth, spreading it with a cherry and breadcrumb filling, and rolling it into a tight cylinder before baking. The stretched dough, not filo from a packet, is what gives a genuine strudel its characteristic flaking, paper-like layers. A demanding technique but the results are distinct from anything achievable with commercial filo.
sourceNotes: Strudel using stretched pastry is documented in Austro-Hungarian and Central European baking literature from at least the eighteenth century. Joseph Schraembl published early documented strudel recipes in Vienna in the 1790s (now public domain). Marie Zelenka, Viennese Cooking (English edition 1902, public domain), describes the stretched-pastry method as the only correct approach. The cherry (kirschen) filling variant is described as a standard summer strudel in Austro-Hungarian domestic cookery guides published before 1928.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten warm from the oven with icing sugar. Leftovers can be reheated in a 170°C oven for 10 minutes. The dough can be made and rested the night before, covered tightly.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 170°C. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until deep golden and the underside is crisp.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pulling the rested strudel dough paper-thin by placing it on a large, well-floured cloth and working the hands underneath the dough, pushing outward from the centre with the backs of the hands rather than pulling from the edges. The dough should eventually be large enough to read newsprint through it and should not tear. Remove rings before stretching as they will puncture the dough. Small tears can be tolerated; large holes will cause the filling to leak. Brush melted butter over the stretched dough immediately before adding the filling to keep it pliable.

content.text: The dough has oil in it so it stretches without tearing. Rest it for 30 minutes before you begin.
takes practice. The first attempt will not be perfect. It will still be far better than anything made with shop-bought filo.

items.prepNote: toasted in butter, to absorb cherry juice

content.text: Mix the flour, warm water, oil, and salt into a smooth, very soft dough. Knead for 5 minutes until satiny. Brush all over with a little oil, place in a bowl, cover with a warm bowl inverted over it, and rest for 30 minutes.

Cover a large table with a clean cloth and flour it generously. Place the rested dough in the centre and roll it with a pin as far as it will go. Then, working with hands beneath the dough, stretch it outward until paper-thin. Brush with melted butter. Scatter the toasted breadcrumbs over two-thirds of the surface, leaving a border. Toss the cherries with sugar, cinnamon, and lemon juice and spread over the breadcrumbs. Using the cloth to help, roll the strudel tightly into a log, tucking in the ends. Transfer to a lined baking tray seam-side down.

Heat the oven to 190°C conventional (170°C fan). Brush the strudel generously with melted butter. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes, brushing with more butter at 20 minutes, until deep golden and the pastry is crisp on the base. Dust generously with icing sugar before serving.
